responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

THE PSYCHOANALYTIC QUARTERLY: An Appreciation

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly has completed twenty-five years of publication. Beginning its career in 1932, at a time when American psychoanalysis was taking large forward steps as an organized profession and when its broadening front required a special forum in this country, it has pursued an illustrious course.

At the time of its founding, the available outlets for the publication of psychoanalytic contributions were few. There were limited media for the publication of psychoanalytic material in English, and little space was available for the publication of work which would represent the rapidly growing body of American psychoanalysis. It was to supply this need that The Psychoanalytic Quarterly was established, devoted, as stated in its first editorial note, to avoiding the "danger of misrepresentation and dilution [of psychoanalysis) with ideas foreign to it, both in respect to theory and method."

Many illustrious names in American psychoanalysis have been associated with the development of the Quarterly: Dorian Feigenbaum, Bertram D. Lewin, Frankwood Williams, Gregory Zilboorg, Henry Alden Bunker, Herbert A. Wiggers, Lawrence S. Kubie. In 1933, Miss Pauline Turkel became affiliated with the Quarterly and in 1934 Raymond Gosselin became one of the Associate Editors. In 1943 Dr. Gosselin became the Editor and Miss Turkel the Executive Assistant. Gradually, over the years, the major burden of publishing the Quarterly has fallen on the shoulders of these two. It is in large part to them that the Quarterly owes its present high quality and standing in the psychoanalytic community.

With keen appreciation of the tremendous expenditure of time, effort and dedication that goes into the successful operation of a scientific periodical, the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association extends its congratulations and best wishes on this Twenty-fifth Anniversary of the Quarterly to all of our colleagues who have brought it to its eminent place in American psychoanalysis. We especially want to express our best wishes to Dr. Raymond Gosselin and Miss Pauline Turkel for many more years of productive work.

Psychoanalysis and Religion11

Nathaniel Ross M.D.

Nathaniel Ross

Nathaniel Ross  

It will occasion no surprise that of the nine books here under consideration, all but one represent various attempts at reconciling psychiatry (and psychoanalysis) with religion. And if the number of such volumes appears formidable for one essay in this section, it could easily be multiplied many times during a span of only the past five years.

This trend toward a synthesis between psychiatry and religion is simply one aspect of the "religious revival" in the contemporary scene. Within the domain of organized psychiatry there are open and powerful affirmations of the desirability of such a rapprochement. The editor of one of the books here discussed has been President of the American Psychiatric Association, while another President of the same organization during 1955-1956 devoted his

[[RunningHead]]

A Note on the Connection Between Preliminary Communications and Subsequently Reported Dreams11

Samuel Lipton M.D.

Samuel Lipton

Samuel Lipton  

Occasionally analytic patients make some comment, at the outset of an hour, which they consider of a preliminary or causal nature and somewhat separate from their usual associations. Such a remark may be made in connection with the salutation, or during the few moments it takes to reach the couch, or is otherwise set aside from the rest of the material. Quite often this remark proves to have a latent content which is closely related to a dream which is reported later in the hour, and one's cognizance of this relationship can lead to clearer understanding. The following clinical examples are offered to illustrate this point.

1. A patient opened his hour by remarking that he had been frightened on his way to his session. He was a little late, had crossed the street against the signal, and had feared that one of the cars, passing close, might hit him. Later in the hour he said he had dreamed the night before but remembered only that he was arguing with me. The dream had been provoked by his reading a book by a man who argued with Freud a great deal during his analysis. He envied this man's daring. He reported a fantasy that he might be attacked by a rejected suitor of a woman he was interested in. The street itself he had crossed hundreds of times without finding it necessary to make any comment about it. His pervasive feeling at this time was that I would punish him for sexual wishes. His initial comment, therefore, meant that he was afraid that I would hit him for defying me. This fear was displaced from me to the traffic and thereby became accessible to

Submitted May 5, 1957

[[RunningHead]]

The Communication of Affect in Rhesus Monkeys:—I. an Experimental Method11

I. Mirsky , Robert Miller and John Murphy

John Murphy

John Murphy  

Every social interaction between men is dependent upon the processes whereby information is transmitted from one individual to another. These processes involve verbal and nonverbal communications. Every therapist is aware that although attitudes, moods and feelings may be conveyed not only through content but also through the tone of the words used in communication between therapist and patient (12), of even greater significance may be the communication of affects through some consciously or unconsciously perceived behavior (8), (24), (26). Whereas the processes of verbal communication have received a relatively large amount of attention, little is known concerning the mechanisms of nonverbal communication.

Many examples of nonverbal communication of feelings and attitudes between animals (2), (28), between men and animals (13), between mother and child (4), (7), (11), (20), and between men (21), (23) can be cited. Yet the mechanism whereby such communication is achieved is unknown. Undoubtedly one determinant in the nonverbal communication of any affect is the perception of subliminal as well as obvious behavioral cues associated with the expression of that affect.

Submitted October 24, 1957

The Initial Psychiatric Interview and the Principles of Psychotherapy; Some Recent Contributions11

Victor Rosen M.D.

Victor Rosen

Victor Rosen  

The initial psychiatric interview as a problem in technique, a phase of therapy and an area of research opportunity has certainly been inadequately exploited by psychoanalysts. Freud (3) made very few comments on the subject of "history taking" as such. His most extended remarks appeared in his 1913 paper, "Further Recommendations in the Technique of Psychoanalysis." These suggestions appear to have influenced a great deal of past and current procedure in regard to this initial interview in psychoanalytic practice. In the beginning of "A Case of Obsessional Neurosis" (2) one notes a short, condensed initial interview which includes more than the chief complaint of the patient. It is reasonable to assume that this was conducted vis-à-vis since the next part of the report is entitled "The Beginning of Treatment." It is also apparent here that Freud felt it permissible to ask for certain direct information beyond what was freely volunteered. If one looked for injunctions rather than suggestions in his paper on technique, it might lead to the suspicion that there had been an interdiction of such previews. In this latter context he says in regard to diagnosis and the selection of suitable cases, "I have formed the practice of first undertaking it [the treatment] only provisionally for one or two weeks. If one breaks off within this period the patient is spared the distress of an unsuccessful attempt at

1Lewis R. Wolberg, THE TECHNIQUE OF PSYCHOTHERAPY. New York: Grune & Stratton, 1954, xiv + 869 pages, $14.75.

Merton M. Gill, Richard Newman, Fredrick C. Redlich (with the collaboration of Margaret Sommers), THE INITIAL INTERVIEW IN PSYCHIATRIC PRACTICE. New York: International Universities Press, 1954, 423 pages, $6.00 (with set of three twelve-inch long-playing phonographic records, combined cost, $10.60).

Felix Deutsch and William F. Murphy, THE CLINICAL INTERVIEW. New York: International Universities Press, 1955. Vol. I: "Diagnosis—A Method of Teaching Associative Exploration, " 613 pages, $10.00; Vol. II: "Therapy—A Method of Teaching Sector Psychotherapy, " 336 pages, $7.50.

[[RunningHead]]

Panel Reports—Superego Development and Pathology in Childhood11

Irving Kaufman M.D.

Irving Kaufman

Irving Kaufman  

David Beres presented a resumé of some of Freud's writing, including a historical perspective of the development of the concepts leading to Freud's description of the superego as a definitive component of the psychic structure, taking its rightful place with the id and the ego. As David Beres said, the clinical papers demonstrated many theoretical concepts, and the theoretical papers gave excellent clinical examples.

Although the various panel members focused on certain specific portions of the subject, the general effect was a multifaceted enriched approach to an understanding of the superego. Some of the areas which were considered included: (1) the phenomena associated with the normal development of the superego; (2) the relationships between the superego and the other structural components of the psyche; (3) the role of the superego in the emotional disturbances of childhood; (4) observations on the changes in the superego as the personality of the patient is effected by therapy; (5) sociologic observations on superego phenomena providing a perspective from another discipline.

In the first paper, "Comments on the Factors Which Determine the Constitution of the Superego as a Functional System, " Edith Jacobson focused on the factors actually responsible for the formation of the superego and its relationship to the resolution of the oedipus conflict. She reminded us of Freud's statement that the period of the resolution of the oedipus complex is the time when the instinctual conflicts of the boy come to a dangerous climax. This is the result of the castration anxiety and the sexual renunciation of the incestuous love object which is enforced with a finality that far surpasses the restrictions imposed on the components of the child's pregenital drive.

1Presented at the Midwinter Meeting, New York, December, 1957. Chairman: David Beres, M.D.540

Dream Resistance and Schizophrenia11

Ives Hendrick M.D.

Ives Hendrick

Ives Hendrick  

I

The primary etiologic factors in schizophrenic conditions are still unknown. But empirical psychoanalysis has shown the schizophrenic's mental processes to have a meaning; and theoretical psychoanalysis has enabled us to formulate the concept of its libidinal mechanism as primarily an introversion of instinctual energy normally expended in relationships with people. It has established the dynamic function of fantasy in mental life, the meaning of sexual symbols, and the dynamics of "narcissism." All these contributions can be summarized in the formula: the dream is the normal prototype of the psychosis. More than thirty-three years ago (in 1900) Freud remarked: "The dream is a fragment of the abandoned psychic life of the child. In the psychoses these modes of operation of the psychic apparatus, which are normally suppressed in the waking state, reassert themselves, and then betray

Submitted December 12, 1956

1This paper is a condensation of two unpublished papers: "Dream Resistance and Schizophrenia, " read at the Joint Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association and the American Psychiatric Association, May 31, 1933, and "The Sexualization of Hostile Trends in Dream Resistance and in Schizophrenia, " read at the 176th meeting of the New York Psychoanalytic Society, April 20, 1935. In spite of the antiquity of these earlier papers, the author has felt justified in presenting this paper; the subject matter still seems "modern, " and the possibilities of this kind of clinical studies and their evaluation do not seem exhausted during the intervening twenty years.

This new version of the two earlier papers is entirely a work of abbreviation and combination, and contains no new bibliographical, clinical, or ideational material. The author will appreciate the reader's having this in mind in such matters as use of the present tense, which refers to 1933 or 1935, not to 1956.

The present version was read at the Boston Psychoanalytic Society, March 21, 1956; and the Midwinter Meeting, American Psychoanalytic Association, December 8, 1956.

responseSet.title: The Role of the Counterphobic Mechanism in Addiction
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Role of the Counterphobic Mechanism in Addiction11

Thomas Szasz M.D.

Thomas Szasz

Thomas Szasz  

SUMMARY

1. The purpose of this paper is to identify and to demonstrate the role and significance of the counterphobic mechanism in addiction. The latter term is used in a primarily descriptive sense and includes phenomena which are both socially accepted and repudiated.

2. The observations reported herein are based on the analytic treatment of one patient who was an alcohol and drug addict in the traditional sense of this term, as well as on observations of two men who used tobacco in a socially approved manner. In addition to the clinical material, some cultural phenomena pertaining to this problem are discussed.

3. The nature and function of the counterphobic mechanism in addiction is illustrated. The sources of the "danger" which is displaced unto, and is symbolized by, the object of addiction are discussed.

4. According to the view put forward, an important mechanism (at least in some cases) of addiction lies in the following. The symptom represents a repetitive ("dramatic") re-enactment of a situation of danger (e.g., fear of instinct, of helplessness or fearful fantasies of bodily damage in connection with pregnancy). The ego deliberately exposes itself to this situation in the hope of achieving mastery. The gratification associated with this process derives from the knowledge that one has been through the danger and has emerged unharmed. Interference with the symptom exposes the ego more directly to the underlying phobic, hypochondriacal and paranoid anxieties.

responseSet.title: Pavor Nocturnus
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Pavor Nocturnus

Melitta Sperling M.D.

Melitta Sperling

Melitta Sperling  

Freud had the fond expectation that child analysis would supply the clinical evidence for concepts arrived at by reconstruction from the analyses of adults. Speaking of pavor nocturnus (2), Freud remarked upon the lack of psychoanalytic child material at the time he put forward his suggestions concerning the nature of this condition in children. In the meantime, the psychoanalytic literature has provided ample confirmation for the correctness of his assumptions.

The interest in a systematic psychoanalytic investigation of sleep disturbances in children is, however, of fairly recent origin (1), (11), (13). This paper, based upon an extensive clinical experience with children and their parents, constitutes such an attempt at systematic psychoanalytic study of pavor nocturnus in children.

Pavor nocturnus is so frequently encountered in children that pediatricians and parents have come to regard it as a phenomenon of childhood to be placed in a category similar to certain daytime fears considered typical in children.

If we consider the fact that the wish fulfillment tendency of the dream is to be observed in young children in an almost undisguised way, the occurrence of a pavor nocturnus is a somewhat surprising finding. We assume that in children the ego and the superego are not so strongly opposed to the demands of the id as in the adult, and will permit fulfillment of frustrated or warded-off wishes to come through in the state of sleep, with little or no objection.

What then causes children to suffer from the sleep disturbance known as pavor nocturnus?

Submitted March 30, 1956

79

[[RunningHead]]

Panel Reports—The Psychology of Adolescence11

Edith Buxbaum Ph.D.

Edith Buxbaum

Edith Buxbaum  

Maurice Friend introduced the panel with a definition of the term from Samuel Johnson's dictionary of the English language in 1785. It says there: "Adolescence is the age succeeding childhood and succeeded by puberty; more largely, that part of life in which the body had not yet reached its full perfection, " and a little further "The sons must have a tedious time of childhood and adolescence before they can either themselves assist their parents or encourage them with new hopes of posterity."

The "tedious time" is what we call the "psychological process of adaptation to the conditions of puberty."

The panel consisted of four papers and their respective discussions. They dealt with early, middle and late adolescence clinically and discussed each phase from a theoretical point of view. Leo A. Spiegel attempted to formulate a general theory of adolescence which formed a common basis for the succeeding papers and discussions. In "Comments on the Psychoanalytic Psychology of Adolescence, " Spiegel deals with adolescence as an epoch in personality development rather than with a study of individuals. His paper is so rich in ideas that only a few can be mentioned. The expectation expressed many years ago by Anna Freud, that the study of adolescence would yield information about the structure of the ego has hardly been fulfilled due to lack of detailed analytic information about this period of life. He emphasizes a point which Root made too, that it is of extreme importance to reconstruct the adolescent phase in every analysis. As a matter of fact, analysis has to be considered incomplete if the patient's adolescence is not thoroughly understood. Friend raised the question of why it is so difficult to deal with this phase of development both in the analysis of adults and during adolescence itself. Adults often shy away from examining and remembering their adolescence, because they still continue it or because they strenuously try to counteract it.

Book Notices

ART AND PSYCHOANALYSIS, edited by William Phillips. New York: Criterion Books, 1957, xxiv + 552 pages, $8.50.

A series of articles, gathered from various publications, upon the relationships between art and psychoanalysis, most of them by psychoanalysts, others by analytically oriented psychologists, sociologists and anthropologists, and a number by novelists and critics. Among the authors represented are Freud, Jones, Greenacre, Fromm, Ernst Kris, Róheim, Thomas Mann, Lionel Trilling and Edmund Wilson. The contributions fall into three general categories: studies of single works of art or creative artists, theoretical essays, and literary pieces. In his Introduction, William Phillips, who is co-editor of Partisan Review, suggests the enormous complexity of the relationship between art and neurosis, designating as the "modern experience … the unique combination of neurotic experience with some apparently objective or plausible view of the world, such as we find in writers like Kafka or Eliot …" He says further, "Our idea of art embraces in addition a control of the medium, a profound and arresting sense of the world, and it is conceivable that this power, though mainly intellectual, is enhanced by a disposition, which we would call neurotic, to reject conventional attitudes."

BEYOND LAUGHTER, by Martin Grotjahn. New York, Toronto, London: The Blakiston Division, McGraw-Hill Book Company, Inc., 1957, ix + 285 pages, $6.00.

The author expands the thesis that "The analytic study of laughter is a study of creative communication between the unconscious and the conscious, leading to the experience of happiness in fulfilling one's potentialities." On the basis of Freud's original findings, he describes the unconscious factors in wit, humor, the humorist as a personality type, the developmental aspects of laughter and humor, and the relationship of the comic to artistic creativity. Laughter in its creative meaning illustrates "our need for free and episodic regression—or communication with our unconscious, as in sleep and dream—in order to gain strength for this reality we live in.

responseSet.title: How Autonomous is the Ego?
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

How Autonomous is the Ego?

David Grauer Ph.D.

David Grauer

David Grauer  

SUMMARY

The concept of ego autonomy introduced by Hartmann and developed by Rapaport is critically examined. In view of Hartmann's modification of Freud's original position on the origin of the ego, it is believed that a more positive statement is warranted regarding the ego's constructive activities than is given by these writers. Hartmann's hypotheses of drive neutralization and secondary autonomy are discussed and criticized. Formulations by Sigmund Freud and by Anna Freud are interpreted as justifying the concept of ego autonomy. A more definite conception of the constructive functions of the ego is presented, utilizing some of the ideas of Federn, Weiss, Angyal, and Menninger.

[[RunningHead]]

The Ego and Motility in Sleepwalking

Zelda Teplitz M.D.

Zelda Teplitz

Zelda Teplitz  

Reconsideration of sleepwalking from the viewpoint of developmental and ego psychology may be of interest both clinically and theoretically. Sleepwalking, still a dramatic and rather puzzling symptom, is also a phenomenon related to the problem of consciousness. The author was fortunate in being able to study seven cases of somnambulism. One was an adult woman referred for somnambulism. She was analyzed and studied over a period of more than five years. Six additional cases in children treated in outpatient clinics also were studied.1

Early studies of sleepwalking had emphasized the relationship to hysteria and hypnotic phenomena. However, the role of the ego in dreams and sleep has been emphasized by French (4) and others. That ego development, self-assertion, and aggression are intimately connected with skilled motor activity such as locomotion and manipulation has been discussed by Hartmann, Kris, and Loewenstein (8) and more recently by Mittelmann (15). In the five recent conferences on problems of consciousness sponsored by the Josiah Macy Jr., Foundation, sleepwalking was touched on only incidentally by Rapaport (16) and Kleitman (12). Rapaport, in a discussion of a case of hysterical amnesia drew comparisons between sleepwalking, amnesia and fugue states. What they have in common is that unconscious wishes are acted out rather than expressed in symbolic form as in dreams. Earlier workers, Ferenczi (3), Coriat (2), and Sadger (17), had also done this.

Submitted March 22, 1956

1Northwestern University Medical School and the Lake County Mental Health Clinic (Gary, Indiana).95

[[RunningHead]]

Education or the Quest for Omniscience11

Bertram Lewin M.D.

Bertram Lewin

Bertram Lewin  

This lecture marks the anniversary of the founding of the Institute for Psychoanalysis in Chicago twenty-five years ago by Franz Alexander. Fortunate Franz Alexander, non sine honore a prophet, who robbed Juvenal's goddess Fortuna of her divinity by his foresight. But this anniversary is not a local matter. It is a significant mark for all who esteem psychoanalysis and are concerned for its teaching and for its progress as a science. It is a moment for retrospection and reflection. I deeply appreciate the honor shown me by the invitation to deliver the First Alexander Lecture and to voice some of the thoughts appropriate to the occasion.

Many come to mind readily which relate to the rapid rise and development of psychoanalytic education. The year 1932, in which the Institute in Chicago was founded, was a good one for psychoanalysis in the United States. For some years previously, many Americans had studied psychoanalysis abroad, and the example of the new institutes there, particularly the one in Berlin, was making a strong impression on this side of the water. In several American cities the psychoanalytic groups, having grown in membership, desired and were better able to organize the education which was necessary for a psychoanalyst. Dr. Alexander was the first distinguished teacher invited to this country from one of the admired foreign institutes. Other signs of the same élan were the similar invitations to other teachers in the older European communities, the founding of the New York Psychoanalytic Institute in the same year 1932, and the first issue of the Psychoanalytic Quarterly in April of that year.

Submitted March 3, 1958

[[RunningHead]]

Superego, Introjected Mother, and Energy Discharge in Schizophrenia: Contribution from the Study of Anterior Lobotomy11

Eugene Brody M.D.

Eugene Brody

Eugene Brody  

SUMMARY

The psychic apparatus of the schizophrenic is conceptualized as an unstable, "high-pressure" energy system with a limited capacity for discharge. A critical feature of this system is the superego, with its introjected maternal constituent, conceptualized as a disordered regulating agency functioning through the intrapsychic release of nonneutralized aggressive energy in a rigid, nonflexible manner. The innate tendency of the system toward organization and integration is evoked in its attempt to maintain an intrapsychic steady state in the presence of a superego which acts as a disturbing foreign body. One expression of this tendency is the schizophrenic's wish for oral unity with his mother.

Anterior lobotomy disrupts (in a nonspecific manner) the schizophrenic pattern of superego function. In men modifications in the manner of dealing with the incest conflict are prominently observed, while in women the manner of dealing with problems of maternal identification is modified. New, more effective and varied avenues of libidinal and aggressive energy discharge become

498

available, associated with more gratification and less disguise of previously prohibited, particularly pregenital and prephallic, instinctual wishes. The system thus becomes less differentiated, more stable, and relatively "low pressure." The threat to the ego of overwhelming energies, including those mediated by the superego, is reduced. Neutralized aggressive energy becomes more available for certain ego functions because of the diminished need to energize the defensive activities of the ego. This allows it to engage in a certain amount of organized action in respect to objects, and to attempt to control objects as a newly available means of controlling drives. A modification in the distribution of object cathexis reflected in reduced danger of destroying objects, of loss of love and of loss of control permits a more effective, narcissistic relationship between self and object. By changing the intrapsychic energy balance, behavior becomes possible which is at best more adaptive and ego-syntonic, and which often may be ego-syntonic without being adaptive.

The schizophrenic superego reorganized after lobotomy into a less differentiated regulating organization is thought of as a narcissistic ego ideal denying the ego's limitations and attempting to regain infantile omnipotence by identifying with the idealized parent through oral incorporation. The relative fusion of ego and superego which occurs is considered equivalent in terms of intrapsychic structure to, in symbolic terms, a state of relative unity with the mother. In this sense the lobotomized schizophrenic is a more successful schizophrenic in that he has achieved what he has always wanted: that oral reunion with his mother previously feared as engulfing or devouring and threatening to his identity.

[[RunningHead]]

Reading and Daydreams in Latency, Boy-Girl Differences11

Lili Peller

Lili Peller

Lili Peller  

The three- to four-year-old child who wishes to be a great general, a doctor or a mother can without delay become that person, possibly supporting his magical thinking (denial in fantasy) with some token, a soldier's cap, a doctor's bag or a doll. He plays with abandon, without self-consciousness. With the beginning of latency this avenue does not become blocked entirely, but it grows narrower. Latency brings several changes: now the playing child adds realistic features to his play, his imagination carries him further and thus his play changes in two dimensions, becoming more realistic and yet more imaginative. But fantasy and reality do not float into one another all the time, as they did in the earlier years. The acting out of fantasies becomes labeled "make-believe play, " with a clearly marked beginning and end. Fantasies more and more replace role play. Within a short time the psychological household grows far more complicated and daydreams become an essential release. Finding his own daydream woven into a story multiplies the release—the child can enjoy his own, his personal fantasy without feelings of guilt, shame, reproach. (This function of "the poet's" work is of course not latency-specific: we watch a play or read a novel in order to reap the same benefits.) Yet the fantasy which originally guided his attention toward the story usually remains the child's secret.

In child analysis the child acts out or relates his fantasies. Sometimes we may gain access to fantasy material through his choice of a story or a joke he tells us. Margarete Ruben (10)

[[RunningHead]]

Early Physical Determinants in the Development of the Sense of Identity11

Phyllis Greenacre M.D.

Phyllis Greenacre

Phyllis Greenacre  

SUMMARY

In this paper I have focused on a limited but crucial area in the beginning of the sense of identity: in the development of the body self-image which is always important and may be peculiarly susceptible to distortion and instability, viz., the awareness of the nature of the own genitals, face, and to a lesser extent the total body form. I have attempted to indicate that even in the early stages of development, there are inherent, in the assembling of the awareness of the body self, an inner and an outer aspect: the one dependent on the intrinsic body-organizational structure and the other on the awareness of the outer surface and form. In the structural organization lies the core of the identity itself, but the awareness of identity, the sense of identity, is influenced very much by factors affecting awareness of external attributes. Primitive as this kind of self-appraisal is, it continues to play a role throughout life. It is always changing and sometimes important.

I would conceive of the sense of the own identity coming into some kind of preliminary working form during the anal phase but reaching special development with the phallic-oedipal period. By that time the child is well aware of himself existing in a world of outer objects, knows he has thoughts and memory, appreciates relative size, but has knowledge of sexual differences, knows the

625

names of his body parts and of himself. He is generally aware of himself as a unit in a group.

It would seem to me that identity, although generally having a stable core both in body and psychic structure and functioning, is nonetheless subject to various changes and nodal points of development, roughly following stages of body and maturational achievement with their accompanying and emotional problems. Consequently, no sense of adult functional identity can be completed until after adolescence is well past and assimilated.

But even then there are further changes occurring later in life, e.g., in sickness, in the involutional period and in senescence. Under most circumstances the sense of the own identity follows with the organizational structural identity but will always remain susceptible to the influence of changes in the individual's relationship to his environment—whether this be in his development within his adult family life, as a member of the community, or in other group relationships. In this overschematic presentation, I have neglected the larger and sometimes rather massive post-oedipal identifications which may repeat themselves in early adolescence and are of such importance in the superego system and the development of "values." Even here, however, I suspect they are influenced very much by these early oedipal forces of identity pressures.

A relatively late realization of important aspects of the own identity certainly may occur in the artist. I believe, however, that even in these instances there is an inner resonance, retrospectively recognizable as early as the phallic phase. It often does not show itself in a functioning way sufficiently to be added clearly to the sense of self until much later. Frequently this occurs in early puberty. The artist, however, hardly seems fair game for our basic formulation, since it seems likely that his relationship to the outer world is of a different texture from that of others, from his earliest days and predisposed to an apparent greater instability. Significantly, too, artists are conspicuously troubled by problems of their own sense of multiple identities and sometimes experience a sudden awareness of abilities which they have not previously

626

recognized in themselves. There is then a revelation of an extension of the self-image.

[[RunningHead]]

The Significance of the External Female Genitalia and of Female Orgasm for the Male

George Devereux Ph.D.

George Devereux

George Devereux  

One of the chief tasks of so-called applied psychoanalysis is to clarify important clinical problems by means of data pertaining to cultural practices in various primitive groups. These data can be treated as equivalents of unconscious material elicited in the course of psychoanalytic therapy, since it has been repeatedly shown that material which is repressed in one culture, such as ours, may be out in the open and culturally implemented in another culture, which need not necessarily be a primitive one.

It is crucial for the understanding of male homosexuality that the sight of the penis-less female genitalia arouses in some men so intense a castration anxiety that coitus with the woman becomes impossible. It is also known that many a man becomes quite anxious if his female partner fails to achieve a climax. We postulate that these two types of anxiety are closely interrelated.

It is proposed, on the basis of Micronesian data, to demonstrate that, in some areas, the anxiety-arousing quality of the penis-less external female genitals is minimized by artificially enlarging them, and that these artificially enlarged genitalia are then manipulated as though they were a penis, in order to elicit in the woman a "masculine" type of orgasm, characterized by urination before and during the climax, which appears to symbolize for the unconscious a kind of female "ejaculation." In brief, it is proposed to discuss the "reassuring" quality of certain anatomical features of the female genitalia, and of an unusual form of orgasm in the female, for the male partner.

It is well known that the problem of vaginal orgasm is still a moot point for some. Thus, Kinsey (16) suggested that vaginal orgasm may be a fiction, since the internal tracts of the female genitalia allegedly contain no organ capable of providing an orgasm.

Submitted August 23, 1957

[[RunningHead]]

Problems of Identity11

David Rubinfine M.D.

David Rubinfine

David Rubinfine  

This panel was organized and developed around three papers presented by K. R. Eissler, Phyllis Greenacre, and Margaret Mahler. It is noteworthy that each paper began with an attempt at a definition of "identity, " and that all of the presentations stressed the significance of the formation of the sexual aspects of the self-image and the resolution of bisexual identifications in the development of the "sense of identity."

The papers by Greenacre and Mahler primarily explored the vicissitudes of identity formation in terms of early object relationships and identifications, against the background of maturational events in infancy and childhood.

To provide a theoretical explanation for certain clinical observations, Eissler suggested that in addition to the three provinces of personality, the id, ego, and superego, there develops another structural differentiation in the ego: the "self." He was further inclined to the view that this fourth province is established fully only at puberty, with genital maturity. While the child becomes partially aware of his identity, these "prestages" do not include the full capacity of the subject to take itself as object, i.e., it has not reached the explicit phase. Eissler maintained that the sense of identity was based on the ego's capacity to experience itself as a continuum. The subject feels he has always existed. The mechanism of repression seems to be a prerequisite for this experience. While the ego's past is subjectively represented in the present, this does not signify that all its experiences are accessible as memories. Extensive amnesias are necessary to this feeling of continuity. Often in schizophrenics whose manifest illness started with a sudden alteration in ego feeling, there is a sense of discontinuity based on all too distinct memories of the time before its onset. Similarly, the return of deeply repressed memories in analysis is often accompanied by feelings of depersonalization. Eissler labeled the "I am I" experience "self-cognizance" and observed that just as the main task of puberty vis-à-vis the instincts is to establish adequate relationships to the opposite sex, so its main task with respect to the ego is to consolidate the ego's relation to itself.

[[RunningHead]]

On Screen Defenses, Screen Hunger and Screen Identity11

Ralph Greenson M.D.

Ralph Greenson

Ralph Greenson  

In the early years of psychoanalysis, patients coming for treatment were suffering from symptom neuroses, a relatively clean-cut and well-defined group of pathological formations. The clinical picture changed as society changed and after World War I, patients seeking therapy were found to be suffering from character disorders, an ill-defined, heterogeneous form of neurosis. Since resuming practice after World War II, it seems to me that once again there is a change in the prevailing clinical picture of patients coming for psychoanalytic treatment. They are still preponderantly character disorders, but now the pathology seems to be centered around a defective formation of the self-image, an identity disorder. The struggle to establish one's self-image or self-representation has been elucidated in recent psychoanalytic literature in the writings of Erikson (5), Jacobson (32), (33), and Hartmann (31). Erikson goes so far as to state that the study of identity today is as strategic as the study of sexuality was in Freud's day. There is no doubt that disorders of the self-image are being described more frequently today (6), (38). In part this certainly is due to our particular interest in studying the vicissitudes of ego development.

Ever since the character disorders dominated the clinical scene, psychoanalysts have focused their attention on the ego functions. The ego's methods and motives in coping with the dangerous instinctual demands and painful reality are the decisive factors in character formation. Among the most important means available to the ego in this struggle are the defenses. The problem of defense is complicated because as yet we have no systematic classification either in regard to chronology or to complexity (16). Freud suggested, however, that it might be helpful to relate particular forms of defense to specific clinical disorders (22). This presentation is an

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Technical Aspects of Regression During Psychoanalysis11

Kenneth Calder M.D.

Kenneth Calder

Kenneth Calder  

The panel discussed two problems of psychoanalytic treatment: how to handle too little regression, and how to handle too much regression. As noted by a number of participants, in psychoanalytic treatment there is an optimum degree of ego regression to the extent that free association and the development of the transference neurosis can occur, yet with limits set so that the regression is at all times reversible. How to achieve this optimum degree of regression was the chief topic. In general, contributors presented cases or groups of cases illustrating the problems under discussion rather than systematic descriptive or diagnostic classification of patients having these two problems of regression. However, Douglass Orr suggested that too little regression was most frequently met in individuals seeking analysis for didactic reasons while too great regression was most common with borderline or psychotic patients. Although technical recommendations for handling these problems were at times specific, all recommendations could be easily subsumed under the general psychoanalytic principle that "you analyze the problem." The contributions can, for convenience, be grouped according to technical problems concerned with too little regression and those concerned with too much regression.

The phenomenon of too little regression was discussed in particular by Morris Brody and by Emanuel Windholz. Brody confined his comments to a special, though important group of analysands, namely, candidates for psychoanalytic training. He noted three problems in this special treatment situation which interfered with adequate regression by the analysand. These three include: (1) façade of normality behind which a number of candidates feel they must hide; (2) countertransference problems in the analyst in attempting a double role of therapist and teacher; and (3) a special intensification of the oedipal conflict wherein the analyst is like a father encouraging the student to explore the mysteries of forbidden territories (symbolically the mother) and the student then fears becoming a rival with the analyst and hence fears facing his own oedipal wishes.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Theory of Thinking
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Theory of Thinking11

Jacob Arlow M.D.

Jacob Arlow

Jacob Arlow  

In opening the panel Leo Rangell pointed out that of the three major expressions of derivatives of the instinctual drives in man, namely, action, affect and thought, the last had been relatively neglected in psychoanalysis. Because of our focus on intrapsychic conflict the content of thought and bizarre disturbances of the thinking process have commanded our attention. The ordinary processes of thought, in their normal development and functional operation, deserve equal consideration. The timeliness of such consideration arises out of the expansion of psychoanalysis into a system of general psychology. In this development, the link with academic psychology must be established and the findings of allied fields assimilated. Furthermore, clinical and experimental work within psychoanalysis (for example, Fisher's experiments on imagery and Rosen's studies of highly gifted abstract thinkers—to illustrate from some of the earlier contributions of members of the panel) are indications of the rising interest in the subject of thought. Finally, Rangell pointed out, our era is an age of remarkable forward movement of human knowledge and power—an era dominated by the threat and promise of a great potential to mankind released by the power of human thinking.

The contribution by Samuel A. Guttman and Paul Sloane entitled "The Development of Freud's Theory of Thinking" served to set the stage for the panel. Fundamentally, these authors emphasized how Freud derived his concepts of thought from considerations of discharge of excitation. Whether he expressed it in terms of the nervous system as in the "Project for a Scientific Psychology" or in terms of the psychic systems as he did in The Interpretation of Dreams, Freud considered the main feature which characterized the totality of mental functioning to be the tendency to keep the level of excitation as low or as constant as possible. Accumulation of excitation caused unpleasure or pain. Discharge of this tension, as, for example, in motor activity, is experienced as pleasurable. When gratification of some need is actually unattainable the memory of earlier gratification is revived, i.e., cathected to such a degree of intensity that hallucination of gratification eventuates. In other words, this hypothecated tendency toward wish-fulfilling hallucination aims to produce a perception identical with earlier experiences of satisfaction.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Study of the Bisexual Meaning of the Foreskin

Brian Bird M.D.

Brian Bird

Brian Bird  

The important role played by the foreskin in the development of mental life has been best delineated by Nunberg (1) in his classic work "Circumcision and Problems of Bisexuality" (1947). In that article he describes several patients whose reaction to loss of the foreskin brought clearly into view many of its myriad meanings. The most significant of these meanings, the one around which all else revolved, was its service in fantasy as an indispensable aid in working out bisexual theories, especially those theories surrounding the origin of life, conception, pregnancy, childbirth, and the difference between the sexes. All of these bisexual thories were found to enter essentially into formation of the castration complex, into symptom structure, and into character development.

Referring to the kind of cases in which bisexual meanings of the foreskin can best be studied, Nunberg considers as useful mainly those patients who were circumcised within the period of their conscious memory, preferably from four to six years of age, and those patients who through identification react to current circumcision of a son. My presentation, however, centers upon a different kind of case, a boy who, using the foreskin as the basis for bisexual theories, developed not only a severe castration syndrome but serious symptoms and character disturbances as well, yet who nevertheless was not circumcised. In this case it seemed to be precisely the fact that he retained his foreskin that led to the formation or at least accentuation of his problem.

When I first saw him, Joey was seven years, nine months old. His brother, six, his sister, three, and his father, a successful young consulting engineer, were all said to be unremarkable. His mother, on the other hand, was very remarkable indeed. She was erratic, petulant, unrealistically ambitious, and falsely optimistic or pessimistic by turns. She herself was currently in analysis.

Submitted May 10, 1956

1Western Reserve University, Cleveland, Ohio.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Theoretical Implications of Hallucinatory Experiences in Schizophrenia11

Arnold Modell M.D.

Arnold Modell

Arnold Modell  

SUMMARY

This paper reported a phenomenological investigation of hallucinatory experiences (predominantly auditory) in schizophrenic patients from which the following major generalizations were drawn:

1. The voices could be identified as formerly loved persons, principally the parents; the hallucinatory experience re-created certain aspects of both wished-for and actual infantile relations to parental objects, referring simultaneously to several stages of infantile development.

2. These hallucinatory objects were closely identified with the self. The voices represented, then, a condensation of both the self and internalized parental objects.

3. Stimulation of erotic zones and discharge of drives was achieved in many cases as a result of listening to the voices. When this phenomenon was present, a definite patterning was observed

where genital stimulation, associated in many instances with oedipal fantasies, appeared to be more prominent than excitation of pregenital zones. This sexual relationship with the hallucinatory object was considered to be narcissistic, as it proceeded no further than relations with an internalized object.

4. Definite differences in the phenomenology of hallucinatory experiences were associated with the sex of the patient: male patients heard predominantly male voices, and the content of the hallucinatory experience related to passive homosexual wishes, whereas female patients heard male and female voices to an equal extent with the content here relating to both active and passive oedipal wishes.

5. In reviewing the nonlibidinized aspects of the hallucinatory experiences, the voices assumed certain executant and judgmental functions that could be related more closely to the ego than to the superego. As such, the patients viewed in the main their relation to the voices as beneficial rather than hostile. These observations were discussed in relation to some recent theories regarding narcissism and the structure of the ego.

6. Some hostile and critical aspects of the relationship of the voices to the patient were observed, and this in turn was considered in regard to the familiar concept that the voices are a type of external superego. A closer examination of these phenomena pointed to the difficulty in differentiating ego from superego process. These critical and prohibitory processes when present were interpreted as relating not to the superego itself, but to a stage in the prehistory of the superego. Therefore, the traditional concept that the voices of schizophrenia represent an externalized conscience could not be confirmed.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Concept of Character11

Arthur Valenstein M.D.

Arthur Valenstein

Arthur Valenstein  

Helen Tartakoff, who chaired the panel, introduced it with a historical review, specifying the contributions of Freud, and their expansion by Jones, Abraham and Ferenczi, and later Anna Freud and Hartmann. She indicated that the original psychoanalytic concept of the basic role of man's instinctual drives and their vicissitudes in character formation has not been outmoded nor substantially revised. However, we have widened our understanding and now appreciate the part played in personality by inborn characteristic ego factors, their early emergence along with instinctual factors, from an "undifferentiated matrix, " the development of both ego and id under the influence of early object relations and their subsequent development in terms of maturation, again under the impact of sociocultural influences. Tartakoff emphasized the fact that these more recent contributions have broadened the psychoanalytic concept of character far beyond its initial framework, i.e., the theory of the neuroses. In concluding her introductory remarks, she sounded the keynote of the panel by stating that the variety of case histories to which we have access in psychoanalytic laboratories now includes not only neuroses and neurotic characters but also any number of so-called normal personalities. As a result, she indicated that we now stand in a position to make substantial new contributions. She added that, in so far as we pursue this goal, we must become more aware than we were in the past of the manifold factors which enter into character formation. Complex genetic and dynamic factors are involved whether the conflicts be solved by reality-adjusted, well-integrated behavior leading to apparent normality or, in contrast, whether a neurotic compromise results, producing overt symptomatology or a character neurosis.

Samuel Atkin then read the first prepared presentation, "A Clinical Investigation into the Nature of Character, " emphasizing both at the beginning of his paper as well as at the close, "the riddle of the total ego, " which he felt escapes definitive psychological formulation and description, but which he nevertheless equated with an axiomatic concept of the psychic functioning of the total organism at the moment of observation. Despite this problem of systematic formulation, he considered the concept of "total ego" as pivotal.

responseSet.title: Counterresistance and Interpretation
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Counterresistance and Interpretation11

Heinrich Racker Ph.D.

Heinrich Racker

Heinrich Racker  

SUMMARY

This paper deals with the resistances which may arise in the analyst against communicating to the patient points he has observed or comprehended. These "counterresistances" indicate (as do the patient's resistances against communicating certain thoughts) the most important conflicts within the patient. For the counterresistances are as a rule the expression of the analyst's identification with the patient's resistances, even though they may at the same time be related to a conflict within the analyst.

The patient's resistance to an interpretation shows, according to Freud, that the latter has been incomplete. Analogously, the counterresistance to giving an interpretation means that the understanding it embodies is as yet incomplete. The cause of these counterresistances thus often lies in the fact that the understanding in question embraced only part of the patient's personality. The understanding may, for instance, have referred to the id, without having taken the ego sufficiently into consideration, or may have referred to an aggressive tendency of the patient's without having included the reaction of his libidinal wishes.

The importance of the analyst's perceiving these counterresistances and overcoming them may be essential, for they are usually responses of his to decisive transference conflicts within the patient. Besides, the postponement of interpretations, deemed premature, also frequently is due to these counterresistances. In such cases, these interpretations are not really "premature, " but simply incomplete. By completing them, considerable loss of time may be avoided.

The means whereby such counterresistances are to be overcome follows from the above: discovering what had been overlooked in the patient's personality, i.e., the cause of the patient's resistance which the analyst had already sensed and echoed in his own counterresistance.

[[RunningHead]]

Technical Aspects of Transference11

David Leach M.D.

David Leach

David Leach  

In his introductory remarks, the chaiman, Jacob A. Arlow, noted that the technical management of transference phenomena is the essence of our daily work. With the broadening of our therapeutic experience and with the deepening of our theoretical understanding, problems of management of the transference have taken on new significance; their meanings reflect in a specific way the consequences of the development of psychoanalysis. The task of the panel was conceived as a step in the direction of further fruitful and rewarding exploration in this area. With illustrations from other areas of psychoanalytic development, Arlow briefly outlined the historical background.

In the first paper entitled "On Transference, " Phyllis Greenacre reviewed and delineated the traditional relationships of analysand and analyst in their two main areas: (1) the transference neurosis, and (2) the therapeutic alliance. She focused especially on the changing relationships within and between these two areas of contact. In this general framework she explored problems under two general headings: (1) transference complications in handling questions of reality with the patient, and (2) certain conditions of what she described as too great fluidity between regressive neurotic manifestations and the critical ego, with the tendency to swamp or crowd out the latter. Greenacre especially noted the necessity of carefully guarding the autonomous attitude of the patient. There were illustrations and clinical examples in reference to the avoidance of interventions, since this weakens the attitude of the patient rather than strengthens it. Sequelae of such interventions were the production of attitudes of special gratitude (or its opposite) which interferes with subsequent transference expression; an increase in the appetite of the patient for such interventions with its accompanying provocations to the analyst; and a playing into the transference with the risk of rearousal of old traumatic experience into which the analyst may be drawn.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Pre-Body-Ego Types of (Pathological) Mental Functioning11

Augusta Bonnard

Augusta Bonnard

Augusta Bonnard  

This communication is an exercise into the realms of the unknowable. The word "unknowable" is here used in a literal sense as follows: not only do we not know what is the quality and type of mental functioning in the first weeks or months of post-birth existence, but we are here attempting to appreciate the impact of phenomena on the infantile mind, before it either knows what they are, or whence they emanate. It is according to this passive designation of phenomena befalling us, that I would wish to place even such aim-directed activities as the infant's initial weeks or months of nuzzling for the nipple, or of crying if the nipple fails to impinge itself in spite of its "seeking" efforts. This example, expressed in quasi-mechanistic or reflex terms, is offered at the outset in order to introduce you to the ontogenetically undifferentiated levels of mental functioning with which this communication sets out to concern itself.

It has been customary to speak of the functions of narcissism, and of libido and its cathexis, as if they were in dynamic operation from the beginning. Instead, it may be more helpful to postulate that while these trends are already larval, especially in the infant in process of normal development, their evolution may be markedly modified if certain more archaic patterns of affectivemental functioning hold sway, instead of being integrated as mere components. If this postulate is correct, then its corollary is worthy of consideration and clinical evaluation.

Submitted October 17, 1957

1First presented at the Beth Israel Hospital, Boston, Mass., in March 1957, and to several psychoanalytic societies in the U.S.A

Presented at the Institute for Psycho-Analysis, London, England, in November 1957.

[[RunningHead]]

Note on One of the Preoedipal Roots of the Superego11

Paul Kramer M.D.

Paul Kramer

Paul Kramer  

In a paper entitled "On Discovering One's Identity" (3) I have tried to show in some detail how in consequence of early preoedipal traumata the discovery of one's self as an entity separate from the mother resulted in a fateful split within the ego, with the isolation of a separate segment from the rest of the ego proper. This segment led an autonomous, in a sense parasitic, existence within the ego organization and played a far-reaching role in the structure of the patient's severe neurosis.

I intend to report today, briefly, and in a preliminary form, some considerations about the faulty development of the superego in the same patient. Before proceeding to the clinical material, and anticipating some of the conclusions from it, I should like to restate in a rough and schematic manner a few theoretical assumptions.

It is agreed that the superego possesses a most complex structure of which no definitive description has yet been given. The literature on the subject is abundant, and an attempt to summarize it is outside of the scope of this note. For the purpose of this presentation only, and in the interest of its simplification, let me assume that the superego is composed of three main parts, and name them, for the sake of convenience, the ego ideal, the prohibiting superego, and the benign superego. The first and the last named are partially overlapping.

Submitted June 22, 1956

1Read at the Panel on Preoedipal Factors in Neurosogenesis, Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Chicago, April 1956.38

[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Contributions on Stuttering: Their Relation to Some Current Insights

I. Glauber M.D.

I. Glauber

I. Glauber  

This report attempts to collate what Freud had written and spoken about the stuttering syndrome; and to review the material in the light of current insights. My aim, as regards subject matter, was not encyclopedic. Actually, the yield was quantitatively not very large, and possibly some material was overlooked. Prior to this study my recollection was that, compared to his contributions on other disorders, Freud's offerings on stuttering were quantitatively small and qualitatively not especially far-reaching. However, when the component fragments dug up were placed together and studied afresh, the material produced a quite different impression. Surprisingly, what Freud dealt with and touched on clinically and theoretically encompassed nearly all the nodal points of the syndrome, despite the fact that his clinical material was in many respects quite atypical. On different occasions he discussed aspects of the genesis and dynamics of the symptom; nosology and differential diagnosis; therapy and prognosis. Furthermore, he contributed observations on some of the special features of stuttering such as, for example, its monosymptomatic appearance, and its relation to earlier ego states. Moreover, some of his observations—to borrow a phrase of his own—struck rock bottom, even though they were often stated in terms we might now consider broad.

Freud had only one case history, as far as I know, in which stuttering played an important role.1 This is the classical case of Frau Emmy von N., one of the five which composed the Studies

Submitted November 5, 1956

1Parenthetically, another important Freudian study involved a man, Moses, who is historically recorded as a stutterer. However, Freud regarded the possibility that Moses was not a stutterer, but one whose speech inhibition might be accounted for on the basis that the prophet was an Egyptian unfamiliar with the Hebrew language (16).326

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Art and Science of Psychoanalysis11

Thomas French M.D.

Thomas French

Thomas French  

In a well-known passage in one of his technical papers, Freud makes some recommendations about how a psychoanalyst should listen to his patient's free associations. "The technique … is a very simple one, " he writes. "It … consists in making no effort to concentrate the attention on anything in particular, and in maintaining in regard to all that one hears the same measure of calm, quiet attentiveness—of 'evenly-hovering attention, ' as I once before described it. In this way a strain which could not be kept up for several hours daily and a danger inseparable from deliberate attentiveness are avoided. For as soon as attention is deliberately concentrated in a certain degree, one begins to select from the material before one; one point will be fixed in the mind with particular clearness and some other consequently disregarded, and in this selection one's expectations or one's inclinations will be followed. This is just what must not be done, however; if one's expectations are followed in this selection there is the danger of never finding anything but what is already known, and if one follows one's inclinations anything which is to be perceived will most certainly be falsified. It must not be forgotten that the meaning of the things one hears is, at all events for the most part, only recognizable later on." Later he adds, "All conscious exertion is to be withheld from the capacity for attention, and one's 'unconscious memory' is to be given full play. … One has simply to listen and not to trouble to keep in mind anything in particular" (3, pp. 324-325).

Submitted September 10, 1957

1Read at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Chicago, May, 1957.2Director of Research, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis.197

[[RunningHead]]

How I Came Into Analysis with Freud

The Wolf Man

The Wolf Man

The Wolf Man  

Introduction by Muriel M. Gardiner, M.D.

Some of you who have read of my earlier meetings with the Wolf Man may be interested to know that I saw him again in January, 1957, the day following his seventieth birthday. He had aged in appearance in the last years, but was as alert and interested as ever. I knew that he had been writing occasional articles, and asked whether he had ever written anything about Freud or about his own analysis. At our next meeting he brought me a manuscript entitled "My Recollections of Sigmund Freud, " of which the following is a translation of about the first third.

I first met Freud in the year 1910. At that time psychoanalysis and the name of its founder were practically unknown beyond the borders of Austria. Before I report on how I came into analysis with Freud, however, I should like to recall to you the desolate situation in which a neurotic found himself at that period before psychoanalysis. A sufferer from neurosis is trying to find his way back into normal life, as he has come into conflict with his environment and then lost contact with it. His emotional life has become "inadequate, " inappropriate to outer reality. His goal is not a real, known object, but rather some other object, hidden in his unconscious, unknown to himself. His affect bypasses the real object, accessible to his consciousness. As long as nothing was known of this state of affairs, only two explanations were possible: one, that of the layman, concerned itself with the increase in intensity of affect, which was out of proportion to the real situation; it was said that the neurotic exaggerated everything.

Submitted May 23, 1957

[[RunningHead]]

On Countertransference "Cures"

Jose Barchilon M.D.

Jose Barchilon

Jose Barchilon  

The phenomenon of countertransference was first described in 1910 by Freud (9) and relatively little was added to it until quite recently, when an abundance of articles has suddenly flourished. These articles concern themselves with the various aspects of the countertransference reaction. A Reich (22) and E. Sharpe (28) emphasized the usefulness of a positive countertransference in the conduct of treatment, while Tower (31) defined the notion of countertransference neurosis, and Weigert (32) used its resolution as one of the signs heralding the end of analysis. In such a context the idea of countertransference cures seems logical, yet I was unable to find any reference to such a concept in the analytic literature.

As the name implies, and in a contrast to transference cures which are due to mechanisms operating entirely in the patient's mind, these cures must occur as a result of unconscious wishes and strivings on the part of the therapist.

Naturally I first became aware of this fact in myself through my own analysis and in my control work with my supervisors. I also noted, like many have before, that some relatively inexperienced psychiatrists in training obtained at times surprisingly good results with a number of very difficult cases. This I generally attributed to their neophyte enthusiasm and regarded as a manifestation of an intuitive understanding of the emotional needs and conflicts of their patients. Such ability is usually considered an early promise of psychotherapeutic skills to be fulfilled by the acquisition of more experience. Fleming, in her article on supervision in psychiatric training (6)

Submitted November 21, 1956

1Associate Professor of Psychiatry, Albert Einstein College of Medicine, New York.222

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Effect of Shedding the First Deciduous Tooth Upon the Passing of the Oedipus Complex of the Male11

Harvey Lewis M.D.

Harvey Lewis

Harvey Lewis  

The thesis of this paper is based upon two early observations made by Freud. The first is that the loss of a tooth in dream or reality has the significance of castration in the unconscious of the adult male. The second, that the unconscious of the adult is derived from the repressed experiences, affects and fantasies of childhood. From these two premises, the following conclusion can be derived: When the adult male reacts to the loss of a permanent tooth (in dream or reality) as if it were a castration, perhaps he does so because when he first shed a deciduous tooth in childhood, it had this same significance at that earlier time.

Three related thoughts follow this synthesis. (i) The oedipus complex is repressed because of castration fear and is succeeded by the latency period at about the age of six. (ii) Freud (31) regarded the passing of the oedipus complex as a biological event. (iii) The shedding of the first deciduous tooth is a biological event which occurs at the same time that the oedipus complex gives way to latency.

We can now state our major hypothesis: "The shedding of the first deciduous tooth is a biological event which makes castration seem like such a reality to the male child, because part of the body falls off without external trauma, that it plays an important role in the compromise solution of the oedipus complex.

Submitted December 5, 1956

1Read at P. & P. I., Michael Reese Hospital, Chicago, November, 1955; before the Western Psychoanalytic Association, Seattle, August, 1956; and the Chicago Psychoanalytic Society, November, 1956.2Research Associate, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis, and Associate Physician, P. & P. I., Michael Reese Hospital, Chicago.5

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Cliché: a Phenomenon of Resistance

Martin Stein M.D.

Martin Stein

Martin Stein  

The cliché1 is best illustrated by the patient who early in her analysis uses the phrase, "mother-figure." She may be referring to a teacher, a supervisor, or, best of all, to her own mother. The analyst is tempted to exclaim, perhaps with irritation, "For heaven's sake, if you are talking about your mother, why don't you come out and say so?" A good question, the answer to which is the intent of this paper.

For the purposes of this discussion, the concept of the cliché will be expanded to include not only stereotyped words or word groups, but also somewhat broader verbal responses. This borders inevitably on a related phenomenon, the use of jargon by patients in analysis. Jargon in this context may be defined as "The cliché use of technical language."2 This paper will deal with both cliché in the ordinary sense and jargon, as they appear in the service of resistance.

A usually inhibited young man described in everyday terms a round of sexual pleasures which he had achieved the night before. He continued, "But I was brought up in a referential system, designed toward the assimilation of a Christ pattern." He was referring to an intense sense of sin derived from his religious upbringing. The use of mathematical circumlocutions was an attempt to relieve the sharpness of his guilt.

Submitted December 17, 1956

1Cliché—" A trite phrase that has lost its precise meaning by iteration; a hackneyed or stereotyped expression" (Webster International Dictionary, 2nd Edition, Unabridged). The word is derived from the French clicher, to stereotype, and in turn, from the German klitsch, a clump or claylike mass, hence, originally to pattern in clay (Webster's New World Dictionary, 1955).2A suggestion for which I am indebted to Lionel Trilling.263

Abstract Thinking and Object Relations—With Special Reference to the Use of Abstraction as a Regressive Defense in Highly Gifted Individuals11

Victor Rosen M.D.

Victor Rosen

Victor Rosen  

The tempo of technological advance in the present century has more than ever stimulated interest in abstract thought, a relatively neglected topic in psychoanalytic psychology. Perhaps the new emphasis in this psychological quest is less upon the process character of abstract theoretical thinking than upon the individual in whom it occurs (15), (16), (21), (22), (38), (39), (41).

There is much popular mythology and folklore about such personalities (a subject so well treated by Ernest Jones [22] in his Freud Anniversary address in New York in 1956). Much of this folklore deserves attention for its fantasy character and has little further interest for the psychologist. The rather widespread view that individuals with unusual intellectual gifts have certain psychological differences from the rest of the population is at least a truism by the very nature of the gift itself although it may be contended that this is one of degree rather than of kind. Recent

Submitted June 13, 1957

1Presented at the annual meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, May 11, 1957, as part of the panel on "The Psychoanalytic Theory of Thinking."

The clinical material in this report was part of the study of gifted individuals undertaken under the auspices of the Treatment Center of the New York Psychoanalytic Institute, with funds made available by the generosity of the Arthur Davison Ficke Foundation. This project was initiated by, and was under the direction of, Dr. Ernst Kris until his death. I am greatly indebted to him and to the following members of the project for the opportunity that it has afforded for a mutual exchange of ideas: Drs. Marianne Kris (the present chairman), Phyllis Greenacre, Annie Reich, Edith Jacobson, Mary O'Neil Hawkins, Margaret Mahler, Leo Stone, Martin Stein, Samuel Ritvo, Leo Loomie, Allan Roos, and Mrs. Christine Olden. The views expressed in this paper are, of course, not necessarily subscribed to by the other members of the group.

2Namely the prevalent idea that the capacity to think is a reward pari passu with the renunciation of instinctual aims.

[[RunningHead]]

Effect of Psychophysiological Research on the Transference

Henry Fox M.D.

Henry Fox

Henry Fox  

The phases in the analytic situation during three years of psychoanalysis were accompanied by alterations in some relatively constant biochemical patterns suggesting a significant correlation (8), (9). The fact that this patient was selected as a research subject which involved psychological tests before and after analysis as well as the daily collection of urine (and regular but less frequent venipunctures) colored the transference in a special manner. The effect on the therapeutic and scientific validity of the psychoanalysis will be discussed as a contribution to the methodology of this type of investigation.

HOMEOSTASIS AND EGO FUNCTION

The analogy between physiological homeostasis and ego function has increasingly attracted the attention of psychoanalysts during the past few years (17), (20), (22). Walter Cannon (2) originally suggested the term homeostasis to designate "the coordinated physiological processes which maintain most of the steady states in the organism." Cannon added that "the word does not imply something set and immobile, a stagnation. It means a condition—a contion which may vary, but which is relatively constant." In Beyond the Pleasure Principle, Freud (12) presented the hypothesis that there is an attempt on the part of the psychic apparatus "to keep the quantity of excitation as low as possible or at least constant." Fenichel (7) emphasized that the ultimate aim for all these equalization tendencies is "the maintenance of a certain level of tension characteristic for the organism." Hartmann (16)

Submitted September 11, 1957

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Comments on the Origin of the Influencing Machine

Louis Linn M.D.

Louis Linn

Louis Linn  

The delusion of being affected by an apparatus which emanates a controlling influence on one's thoughts and actions is a well-known schizophrenic thought formation. The classic paper on the subject was published by Victor Tausk in 1919 (4). In it he details the most frequently encountered characteristics of the influencing machine:

1. It may make the patient see pictures.

2. It may produce thoughts or feelings by means of waves, rays or mysterious forces, or it may "drain off" thoughts or feelings.

3. It may produce a variety of motor phenomena, such as erections, clitoral movements, twitchings of limbs, etc.

4. It may be responsible for physical illnesses like cutaneous eruptions, abcesses, etc.

5. The purpose of the machine is usually to torture the patient, and it is operated by enemies.

6. The operator is exclusively of the male sex and predominantly a physician by whom the patient has been previously treated.

7. The patient can never explain the nature of the machine itself.

8. Physical and ideational psychotic experiences always appear first. The patient then ascribes these to some outsider. The delusion of the influencing machine seems to originate in the patient's need for causality. Its appearance marks what is already a late stage in the evolution of the schizophrenic process.

I have listed these characteristics in some detail because our patient exhibited them with extraordinary completeness.

[[RunningHead]]

Disturbances in Abstract Thinking and Body-Image Formation

Sylvan Keiser M.D.

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser  

A real or fancied impairment of the ability to learn, to know, to do abstract reasoning or to correlate facts has been observed many times as a transitory or minor symptom during the course of an analysis. An opportunity to study this problem as a major complaint was presented to me by several patients during their analyses. It was necessary to evaluate the degree of objectivity in this complaint.

To support the contention that they had a real handicap, these patients argued that pure memorizing, without any understanding of principles or theory, had been responsible for their distinguished scholastic records. In addition to brutally arduous application to memorizing, they employed complicated techniques in preparing for examinations. Later, they learned that reasoning and understanding made it possible to learn with much less effort. They insisted that they had not utilized any principles of association, and that all the facts they had studied simply melted away after the completion of examinations. To substantiate their claims further, they described themselves as unable ever to recall data spontaneously when called upon to initiate a discussion. The sight of a question on the examination would stimulate a "reflex response" in their "mechanical brains" and the answers would be given without thought. Even the most theoretical of concepts were somehow reduced to concrete forms, whose details could be mentally catalogued and filed as a series of facts.

Submitted May 10, 1957

1Division of Psychoanalytic Education of the University of the State of New York, College of Medicine at New York.628
