[[RunningHead]]

The Importance of Characteristics of the Parents in Deciding on Child Analysis11

Isidor Bernstein M.D.

Isidor Bernstein

Isidor Bernstein  

As the knowledge of ego psychology increased and skill in analytic technique advanced, indications for analysis widened to include, besides the transference neuroses, the character disorders—that is, those involving major modifications of the ego. A similar extension of therapy has occurred in the analysis of children. As indications for adult analysis, we consider many psychological characteristics; of particular importance, I believe, are the following:

1. Ability to Associate

Dynamically, this implies that the pressure of unconscious material, aided by the wish to communicate, is strong enough to overcome the resistances. This, in turn, is dependent on the capacity of the ego to ally itself with the analyst, to detach itself to some degree and observe, report and recognize ideas and affects as they emerge from the unconscious. This ability to associate may be rather limited in the beginning phase of the analysis.

The analytic work is accomplished through the wish of the patient to be free of his neurotic suffering and ego limitations. This is aided by the positive feelings in the transference and the recognition of the objective attitude of the analyst. This wish to be helped and the feelings toward the analyst enable the analysand to bear the disapproval of the superego, to face the derivatives of unconscious drives and affects and to suffer the narcissistic injuries involved in reporting infantile material.

Submitted June 1, 1956

1Presented to the Panel on "Indications and Goals of Child Analysis as Compared with Child Psychotherapy" at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Chicago, April 27, 1956.71

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Preadolescent Drive Organization

Peter Blos Ph.D.

Peter Blos

Peter Blos  

It has always been a basic thesis of psychoanalysis to comprehend human behavior at any stage in relation to preceding and current events, or to view it as an instant in a continum of experience. This evolutional concept has thrown light on those complex processes of adolescence which in earlier days remained isolated observations linked solely to the advent of sexual maturation. The genetic approach made adolescent behavioral phenomena tell something about their nature by revealing something of their history.

The "Three Essays" (6) established once and for all the broad sequential pattern of psychosexual development; it furthermore brought into focus the biphasic nature of sexual development as characteristic of man. The early phases of drive development and zonal organization have been explored in detail and have lately become more precisely coordinated with the formation of psychic structure. It is noteworthy to mention that the second broad phase in psychosexual development, namely puberty, while explored in its larger aspects, still awaits a comprehensive theory and the elaboration of sequential patterns. It is far from me to make up for this lack. In the material which I shall lay before you I will attempt, however, to integrate observation and theory in a small sector of adolescent psychology, namely preadolescence.

The familiar cliché which speaks of adolescence as a "second edition" or a "recapitulation" of early childhood has meaning only as far as it emphasizes the fact that adolescence contains elements of preceding phases of development in much the same way as any other phase of psychosexual development is significantly influenced by preceding drive and ego development.

Submitted July 1, 1957

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Comparison of Individual and Group Psychology11

Alfred Stanton M.D.

Alfred Stanton

Alfred Stanton  

The two sessions of the meeting on group psychology were devoted respectively to a consideration of the implications for psychoanalytic theory of current study of group phenomena and to a consideration of the clinical problems implicit in concomitant group and individual treatment.

Leo Berman read a scholarly study of certain "Problems in Working Toward an Integration of Individual and Group Psychology." It is unwarranted, he noted, to believe either that more complete understanding of individual psychology will allow for understanding the group, or that it is never sound to try to conceptualize the human organism in isolation from its social environment. But while there is more and more interest in group therapy and the study of natural groups such as the family, there has been little progress toward a psychoanalytic group psychology in recent years. Berman suggests that some aspects of psychoanalytic theory, especially when exaggerated, are not encouraging to the development of group psychology which is needed.

Berman took up four theoretical issues in turn. The first of these was the value of more attention to the less structured areas of mental functioning. According to Rapaport, an emergent organization is one that has laws of its own distinct from the elements which constitute it; such emergent organizations partly control and are partly controlled by the underlying organization from which they emerge. Thus the autonomy of the ego can only be a relative autonomy; one of its crucial autonomous functions is the internalization of reality, with a consequent ideational representation of reality within the psychic apparatus.

This concept points up certain difficult theoretical problems. He discussed the question of whether there are aspects of the environment which are not the usual grist for the mill of internalization but upon which the person is dependent. He cited Hebb's observations to indicate the degree to which humans seem to be dependent upon external stimuli for their continuing equilibrium. Hebb's subjects submitted to several hours, or a few days, of being treated in such a way as to reduce to a practical minimum the degree of stimulation.
